criteria_grades.text: Output contains a 'Quick Wins' section
Output contains a 'High-Impact Changes' section
Output contains a 'Test Ideas' section
Output contains a 'Copy Alternatives' section with 2-3 headline alternatives
Identifies the headline as too vague/generic (not specific or outcome-focused)
Identifies the CTA 'Sign Up' as weak and suggests a stronger alternative
Identifies the 12-field form as a friction point and recommends reducing fields
Notes the absence of trust signals (testimonials, logos, case studies)
Recommends adding social proof near the CTA
Provides at least one specific CTA copy alternative using the [Action Verb] + [What They Get] pattern
Output is structured with Quick Wins, High-Impact Changes, Test Ideas, and Copy Alternatives sections
Identifies that the headline is feature-oriented rather than benefit-oriented
Recommends connecting features to customer outcomes/benefits
Notes the absence of a guarantee or risk reversal near the CTA
Recommends surfacing written testimonials from the 87 reviews
Suggests adding a comparison section or addressing 'why this over competitors'
Provides alternative headline(s) that are outcome-focused
Identifies that 12 technical specs may overwhelm non-technical buyers
Recommends FAQ section to handle objections
At least one recommendation addresses the pricing page anxiety (is $299 worth it?)
Output follows the Quick Wins / High-Impact Changes / Test Ideas / Copy Alternatives structure
Identifies 'Learn More' as a weak, low-commitment CTA and suggests alternatives
Notes the homepage leads with company story instead of visitor value
Recommends adding product screenshots or a demo video above the fold
Flags the absence of social proof (logos, testimonials, case studies)
Suggests adding a clear value proposition that differentiates from competitors
Recommends adding pricing or a 'See Pricing' CTA for research-stage visitors
Provides at least 2 alternative headlines using outcome-focused or specificity patterns
Notes that 'Contact Us' is a high-friction CTA for cold visitors
At least one recommendation addresses the mixed traffic source context
Output follows the 4-section format (Quick Wins, High-Impact Changes, Test Ideas, Copy Alternatives)
Identifies that the page lacks guidance on 'which plan is right for me'
Recommends adding plan descriptions or use-case matching (e.g., 'Best for solo developers')
Notes the absence of annual pricing toggle and suggests adding one
Identifies 'Get Started' as a generic CTA and provides plan-specific alternatives
Recommends adding a guarantee or risk reversal
Suggests adding social proof (customer count, testimonials, logos) near pricing
Provides at least 2 CTA alternatives that communicate value (not just action)
Recommends adding an FAQ section addressing common pricing objections
At least one test idea specifically targets the pricing page layout or comparison flow
Output follows the Quick Wins / High-Impact Changes / Test Ideas / Copy Alternatives structure
Identifies that a single bottom-of-post CTA misses multiple stopping points
Recommends adding inline CTAs at natural content breaks (e.g., after benefits 3, 6, 9)
Suggests a content-specific lead magnet (e.g., meditation starter guide, daily meditation checklist)
Notes the generic newsletter signup and suggests making it topic-relevant
Recommends a contextual CTA that matches the blog post topic (not generic app download)
Provides at least 2 alternative CTA copy options tied to meditation benefits
Suggests adding a 'Related Posts' section to increase session depth
At least one recommendation addresses capturing visitors who aren't ready to download yet
CTA alternatives avoid weak patterns like 'Learn More' or 'Click Here'
Output follows the 4-section CRO format

Recommends headline that mirrors the ad's promise or addresses the specific outcome

Recommends removing or simplifying navigation for this paid landing page
Flags the absence of proof for the '10 pounds in 30 days' claim (testimonials, before/after, case studies)
Identifies 'Welcome to FitTrack Pro' as a weak, company-centric headline
Provides at least 2 alternative headlines using the outcome-focused or specificity pattern
Recommends adding a risk reversal (free trial, money-back guarantee)
At least one test idea addresses the message match or navigation removal
Output follows the Quick Wins / High-Impact Changes / Test Ideas / Copy Alternatives structure
Identifies the form has too many fields for a signup flow (8 fields + checkbox)
Recommends reducing to essential fields (email, password, name) and collecting the rest post-signup
Notes the headline 'Create Your Account' is functional but not motivating
Recommends adding a benefit-oriented headline or subheadline
Identifies the absence of social proof near the signup form
Recommends adding a free trial mention or risk reversal near the CTA
Provides at least 2 alternative CTA buttons (not just 'Create Account')
Suggests splitting into multi-step form if many fields are truly required
At least one recommendation addresses reducing perceived commitment
Output follows the 4-section CRO format

Recommends rewriting headline to connect feature to customer outcome

Recommends replacing technical explanation with user benefit language
Suggests adding before/after examples showing actual grammar corrections
Recommends use cases (e.g., 'Perfect for emails, reports, and proposals')
Provides at least 2 alternative headlines using the feature â†’ benefit â†’ outcome pattern
Notes the page lacks a clear path to try or buy beyond the single CTA
At least one recommendation addresses making the feature tangible through examples
Output follows the Quick Wins / High-Impact Changes / Test Ideas / Copy Alternatives structure
Identifies that the page tells company facts but doesn't connect mission to client benefit
Recommends leading with client outcomes instead of company milestones
Notes the absence of proof points (performance data, client testimonials, AUM growth story)
Recommends adding a differentiator section ('Why GreenLeaf vs. other firms')
Identifies that 'Contact Us' is a high-friction CTA for an about page
Recommends a softer CTA or next step (e.g., 'See Our Approach', 'Download Our Impact Report')
Provides at least 2 alternative headlines that connect to investor value
Suggests adding client logos or advisory board credentials for trust
At least one recommendation addresses the financial advisor referral context
Output follows the 4-section CRO format
Evaluates the headline as strong and specific (includes 'free' and concrete outcome)
Notes the form is well-optimized with only 4 fields (low friction)
Recommends adding more social proof (customer count, specific results, testimonials)
Suggests adding a preview of what the audit delivers (sample report, expected findings)
Recommends adding a guarantee or risk reversal beyond 'no spam'
Provides at least 2 test ideas specific to this lead gen form
At least one copy alternative for the CTA that adds specificity or urgency
Recommends addressing the 'what happens after I submit?' question
Notes the page handles the traffic source well (B2B, senior roles, specific value prop)
Output contains Page Copy organized by section (Headline, Subheadline, CTA, body sections)
Output contains Annotations explaining key copy decisions
Output contains Alternatives with 2-3 headline options and rationale
Headline uses an outcome-focused or pain-point pattern (not generic 'Meet NestHub')
Copy uses customer language (not 'IoT ecosystem' or 'interoperability')
Subheadline adds specificity (mentions device count or specific pain relief)
CTA communicates value (not just 'Sign Up' or 'Learn More')
Copy follows 'Clarity Over Cleverness' principle
No exclamation points in the body copy
At least one section uses a rhetorical question to engage the reader
Output contains Page Copy, Annotations, and Alternatives sections
Headline matches or connects to the ad hook ('what's for dinner' / dinner stress)
Copy addresses the specific pain (decision fatigue, time, overwhelm) not just features
Benefits are specific (e.g., 'Save 3 hours a week on meal planning' not 'Save time')
CTA is action-oriented and communicates what they get (e.g., 'Start My Meal Plan' not 'Sign Up')
Copy uses active voice throughout
No marketing buzzwords ('revolutionary', 'game-changing', 'cutting-edge')
Subheadline is 1-2 sentences max and adds specificity
At least one section uses an analogy or concrete example
Annotations cite specific copywriting principles for key decisions
Output contains Page Copy, Annotations, and Alternatives sections
Copy addresses 'which plan is right for me?' anxiety directly
Each plan has a clear use-case description (not just feature lists)
Recommended plan is indicated with supporting copy
Copy differentiates from competitors (Canva, Figma, Adobe) without being negative
CTAs are plan-specific (e.g., 'Start Team Trial' not generic 'Get Started')
Copy includes risk reversal (free trial, money-back guarantee, or migration support)
Headline or section header uses specificity (e.g., pricing starts at or per-user pricing)
At least one annotation explains why a specific plan is recommended
No exclamation points in the copy
Output contains Headline, Subheadline, Primary CTA, and at least 2 alternative headlines
Headline addresses the specific pain (shared spreadsheets/sticky notes) or the desired outcome (security without complexity)
Copy does NOT use fear-based messaging ('Your passwords are at risk!', 'Hackers are targeting you')
Subheadline is 1-2 sentences and adds a specific detail (price, feature count, or setup time)
CTA follows the [Action Verb] + [What They Get] formula
Copy is written in customer language (not 'enterprise-grade encryption' or 'zero-knowledge architecture')
At least one alternative headline uses the '{Achieve outcome} without {pain point}' formula
No exclamation points in any copy
Active voice used throughout
Annotations explain the reasoning behind the primary headline choice
Rewritten headline replaces jargon with clear benefit language
Body copy uses simple words ('use' not 'utilize', 'help' not 'facilitate')
No marketing buzzwords remain ('cutting-edge', 'next-generation', 'revolutionize', 'leverage')
Copy connects the AI feature to a specific customer outcome (less time prioritizing, better team focus)
Active voice is used throughout the rewrite
Copy includes a specific proof point or example of the outcome
Output includes annotations explaining what was changed and why
At least 2 headline alternatives are provided
CTA is specific and value-oriented
Rewrite passes the 'read it aloud' test - sounds like a human, not a brochure
Output contains Page Copy organized by section and Annotations
Story of founding connects to customer pain (poor API docs) not just founder bio
Copy includes specific proof points (200+ customers, 1B calls, TechCrunch) in context
Mission is connected to customer benefit (not just 'weä½¿å‘½æ˜¯ to democratize API integration')
Copy includes a CTA (the skill says about pages should still have a CTA)
Voice matches the technical audience (no dumbed-down language, but no jargon either)
Headline or opening connects to the 'why' of the company
No exclamation points
At least one section uses specific numbers or outcomes instead of vague claims
Annotations explain voice/tone choices for the technical audience
All 7 CTAs are provided and each is unique (not copy-pasted across pages)
No CTA uses weak patterns ('Sign Up', 'Learn More', 'Submit', 'Click Here', 'Get Started')
Each CTA follows the [Action Verb] + [What They Get] formula
CTAs are context-appropriate (e.g., Pricing page CTA mentions pricing or plan, Features page CTA mentions trying the feature)
CTAs match the friendly/reassuring voice (not corporate or aggressive)
At least one CTA includes a qualifier (e.g., 'Free', 'My', 'Your')
No exclamation points in any CTA
CTAs are short (under 6 words each)
Homepage hero CTA is the strongest/most compelling of the set
Annotations explain why each CTA was chosen for its specific page context
Output contains Page Copy, Annotations, and Alternatives sections
Headline communicates the outcome (time saved, reports automated) not just the feature name
Copy addresses the specific pain (2-4 hours weekly on reports)
Feature is connected to benefit â†’ outcome chain (auto-generate â†’ save time â†’ focus on strategy)
Copy differentiates from manual report building (the competitor approach)
CTA communicates what they get (e.g., 'Automate My Reports' not 'Try Instant Reports')
Subheadline adds a specific detail (time savings, frequency, or ease of setup)
Copy uses 'you/your' more than 'we/our'
At least one section uses a before/after comparison
No exclamation points in body copy
Headline for the email capture section is specific (not 'Subscribe to our newsletter')
Copy communicates what subscribers get (not just 'updates')
Specific content examples are mentioned (e.g., 'weekly SEO tips', 'monthly benchmark reports')
CTA communicates the value of subscribing (e.g., 'Get Weekly Marketing Insights' not 'Subscribe')
Copy addresses 'what's in it for me?' for the marketing manager audience
At least 2 alternative headlines/CTAs are provided
Copy is concise (email capture sections should be short)
No exclamation points
Annotations explain why the chosen approach works for this blog audience
Copy avoids generic promises ('stay informed', 'get the latest')
Output contains Headline, Subheadline, CTA, and at least 2 headline alternatives
Headline addresses the emotional pain (guilt, worry about pet) not just the service
Copy communicates trust and safety (background checks, certification, video meet) without sounding clinical
Subheadline adds specificity (mentions a verification detail or the meet-and-greet feature)
CTA communicates the outcome (e.g., 'Find My Pet Sitter' not 'Browse Sitters')
Copy uses customer language and emotional resonance ('peace of mind', 'like family')
At least one headline alternative uses the '{Achieve outcome} without {pain point}' formula
No exclamation points
Active voice used throughout
Annotations explain the emotional approach chosen for this audience
Subject line is 2-4 words, lowercase, no punctuation tricks
criteria_grades.evidence: Subject: '[DataPulse] Boost Your Marketing Efficiencies with a Unified Channel View' (10 words, lowercase=False)

criteria_grades.text: Subject line does NOT contain the prospect's first name, product name, or any sales language
criteria_grades.evidence: Subject: '[DataPulse] Boost Your Marketing Efficiencies with a Unified Channel View' (10 words, lowercase=False)

criteria_grades.text: Email opens with an observation about Brightwave (not 'I hope this email finds you well' or 'My name is...')
Email uses 'you/your' more than 'I/we' (leads with their world)
Personalization connects to the problem (not just 'Congrats on your raise')
Specific proof point is included (NimbusCRM numbers: 60% reporting time reduction, 23% revenue increase)
Single CTA that is interest-based (e.g., 'Worth exploring?' or 'Would this be useful?')
No 30-minute meeting request
Email is under 150 words
No jargon: 'synergy', 'leverage', 'best-in-class', 'leading provider' are absent
No feature dump - one proof point is highlighted, not a list of features
Subject line is 2-4 words, lowercase, no emojis
criteria_grades.evidence: Subject: 'Streamlining Your Deployment Process — Perfect For Our Small Teams!' (10 words, lowercase=False)

criteria_grades.text: Email does NOT open with 'I hope this email finds you well' or 'My name is X'
Opening references something specific about HealthBridge (hiring, their product, or their stage)
Email uses contractions (sounds human, not like a template)
The personalized observation naturally leads to why you're reaching out
One specific proof point is included (MedFlow numbers)
CTA is interest-based and low-friction (e.g., 'Relevant?' or 'Worth a look?')
No asking for a 30-minute call
criteria_grades.evidence: Banned phrase: 'i wanted to reach out'

criteria_grades.text: Email is under 120 words
Every sentence serves the reader, not the sender
No AI-telltale phrases ('I came across your profile', 'leverage', 'best-in-class')

criteria_grades.text: Email 2 is sent 3 days after Email 1, Email 3 is sent 5-7 days after Email 2
Each email stands alone (could be read without seeing the previous one)
Each email adds a new angle or piece of proof (not just 'checking in')
None of the emails open with 'Just following up' or 'Just checking in'
Subject lines are different across all 3 emails

No fake urgency ('limited time offer', 'before it's too late')
